Paracelsus is one of the old philosophical authorities Victor studies in his youthful search for nature's secrets.
Walton's polar expedition survives confinement in ice, rescues a suffering European, and gives Walton the intimate friend he long desired; he now records Victor's cautionary history.

Victor is the rescued Genevese stranger pursuing a gigantic being across the polar ice. He tells Walton how a loving childhood, consuming curiosity, and youthful devotion to obsolete natural philosophy began the course that ruined him.
Victor's father is an honorable former Genevese public servant who searched for his ruined friend Beaufort, protected Caroline after Beaufort's death, and later married her.
Victor's unnamed younger brother is his parents' second son and is seven years younger than Victor.
Walton's brave lieutenant helps question the rescued stranger and learns that he is pursuing another sled traveller.
Walton's generous shipmaster helps persuade the nearly frozen stranger to accept rescue aboard the expedition vessel.
Henry's father refuses to let him accompany Victor to university because he distrusts his son's ambitions and favors a commercial life.
Krempe is an uncouth but knowledgeable professor of natural philosophy at Ingolstadt.
Krempe is an uncouth but knowledgeable professor of natural philosophy at Ingolstadt. Krempe dismisses Victor's alchemical education as obsolete and directs him to begin his scientific studies anew.
Waldman is a benevolent chemistry professor whose appearance, voice, and teaching contrast favorably with Krempe's.

Waldman is a benevolent chemistry professor whose appearance, voice, and teaching contrast favorably with Krempe's. Waldman describes the practical achievements and expansive powers of modern chemistry.

Waldman is a benevolent chemistry professor whose appearance, voice, and teaching contrast favorably with Krempe's. Waldman describes the practical achievements and expansive powers of modern chemistry. Waldman advises Victor to study every branch of natural philosophy, shows him his laboratory, recommends books and equipment, and offers future use of his machines.
Caroline nurses Elizabeth through scarlet fever, contracts the illness herself, and dies after expressing hope for Victor and Elizabeth's union and entrusting the younger children to Elizabeth.
Henry Clerval's narrow-minded merchant father rejects his son's academic ambitions and refuses to let him accompany Victor to university.
Elizabeth survives scarlet fever through Caroline's care, then suppresses her own grief to comfort the bereaved family and assume new domestic responsibilities.
Henry wants to join Victor at Ingolstadt and obtain a liberal education, but his father refuses and expects him to remain in commerce; Henry quietly resolves to resist that confinement.
Professor Krempe is an uncouth but knowledgeable Ingolstadt natural philosopher who condemns Victor's alchemical education, directs him toward modern study, and later contributes useful instruction.

After Caroline's death, Victor studies at Ingolstadt, where Waldman's teaching renews his scientific ambition. He masters modern chemistry, discovers how to animate lifeless matter, and obsessively constructs an eight-foot human being, sacrificing his health, relationships, and peace of mind as the work nears completion.
Victor's father sends him to the University of Ingolstadt and later responds to his troubling silence with patient but increasingly pointed inquiries about his work.
M. Waldman is a benevolent chemistry professor whose praise of modern science rekindles Victor's ambition; he becomes Victor's trusted mentor and friend, guiding his broad scientific education.
Justine is the third of the Moritz family's four children and entered Victor's household at age twelve after Caroline intervened against her mother's mistreatment.
M. Moritz dies after having favored Justine among his children.
Madame Moritz mistreated Justine after her husband's death and eventually allowed Caroline to take the girl into her household.

Justine is the third of the Moritz family's four children and entered Victor's household at age twelve after Caroline intervened against her mother's mistreatment. Justine is grateful, frank, cheerful, and devoted to Caroline, whose speech and manners she imitates.

Justine is the third of the Moritz family's four children and entered Victor's household at age twelve after Caroline intervened against her mother's mistreatment. Justine is grateful, frank, cheerful, and devoted to Caroline, whose speech and manners she imitates. Justine attends Caroline with anxious devotion during Caroline's fatal illness.

Justine is the third of the Moritz family's four children and entered Victor's household at age twelve after Caroline intervened against her mother's mistreatment. Justine is grateful, frank, cheerful, and devoted to Caroline, whose speech and manners she imitates. Justine attends Caroline with anxious devotion during Caroline's fatal illness. Grief has softened Justine's former vivacity; Elizabeth now finds her clever, gentle, and very pretty. After her mother's death, Justine returns to Victor's family in Geneva.

Madame Moritz mistreated Justine after her husband's death and eventually allowed Caroline to take the girl into her household. After her other children die, Madame Moritz recalls Justine home but alternates between asking forgiveness and blaming her for their deaths. Madame Moritz dies after a decline during the previous winter.
William is tall for his age, healthy and cheerful, with blue eyes, curling hair, and dimples. William is Victor's young brother living with the family in Geneva.
Caroline was Elizabeth's beloved aunt and Justine Moritz's protectress, bringing the mistreated girl into the household, educating her, and inspiring her devoted imitation and care.
Henry's commercially minded father finally permits his son to pursue a university education after Henry's persistent appeals.
Victor's gigantic constructed being comes alive with a horrifying appearance, approaches him beside his bed, and then disappears from the apartment.
Elizabeth anxiously awaits Victor's recovery, persuades his father not to undertake the dangerous journey to Ingolstadt, and writes urging Victor to reassure the family.

Henry wins permission to study at Ingolstadt, where he devotes months to nursing Victor and discreetly protecting him from distressing reminders. He studies oriental languages for an ambitious eastern career and restores Victor through affectionate companionship.

Justine Moritz is a mistreated daughter whom Caroline welcomed and educated from age twelve. Devoted to Caroline through her fatal illness, Justine later endured her mother's renewed abuse and has now returned to Victor's family, gentler after her grief.
Krempe now praises Victor's extraordinary scientific progress and regards his former novice as the university's leading student.
M. Moritz was Justine's father and favored her among his four children before his death.
Madame Moritz is Justine's widowed mother, who mistreated her, recalled her after the deaths of her other children, and alternated remorse with cruel accusations before dying during the winter.

Victor animates his constructed being but immediately abandons it in horror. A prolonged nervous fever follows; Henry nurses him back to health, and Victor replaces his now-intolerable scientific work with oriental studies before recovering his former cheerfulness.
Victor's father remains healthy and eager for Victor's return; Elizabeth prevents him from travelling to Ingolstadt, and he opposes Ernest's proposed foreign military career.
Victor's sixteen-year-old younger brother is now identified as Ernest. Active and impatient with study, he wants to enter foreign military service despite his family's objections.
William is Victor's cheerful young brother in Geneva, a healthy child with blue eyes, curling hair, and dimples.
Justine's confessor threatens her with excommunication and damnation until she makes a false confession.
Victor's gigantic creation reappears near the site of William's murder and escapes over Mont Salêve; the sight convinces Victor that the being killed his brother.
Elizabeth is devastated by William's murder and briefly blames herself. She steadfastly defends her beloved companion Justine, but her testimony and appeals cannot prevent Justine's execution.
Henry grieves over William's murder, offers Victor heartfelt sympathy, and parts from him when Victor immediately returns to Geneva.

Justine is arrested and condemned for William's murder after Caroline's miniature is found in her pocket. She explains her innocence and reveals that religious coercion produced her false confession, but she is executed despite Victor's and Elizabeth's appeals.
Justine's confessor pressures the condemned woman with threats of excommunication and damnation until she falsely confesses to William's murder.

Victor returns to Geneva after William's murder, sees his creation near the crime scene, and becomes convinced it is the killer. Fearing disbelief, he conceals this knowledge while defending Justine; her execution leaves him consumed by responsibility, remorse, horror, and despair.
Alphonse Frankenstein finds his young son William murdered after a nightlong search and is left overwhelmed by the family's loss.
Ernest is Victor's active sixteen-year-old brother; after losing William during their game, he searches for him and later grieves with the family.
William, Victor's cheerful young brother, is found strangled near Plainpalais after disappearing during a game with Ernest.

Victor's creation approaches him across the glacier with superhuman agility and reveals fluent speech and reasoning. He claims misery and rejection corrupted his original benevolence, demands that Victor hear his history, and threatens further violence if his creator refuses his conditions.
Justine's wrongful execution leaves Elizabeth despondent and distrustful of human justice, though she continues to comfort Victor and appeal to his love for their surviving friends.

Victor sinks into guilt, isolation, and suicidal despair after Justine's death but remains alive to protect his family. Seeking relief in the Alps, he confronts and attacks his creation, then recognizes possible duties toward it and agrees to hear its story.
Alphonse's health is deeply shaken by the family's losses, yet he tries to guide Victor away from excessive grief and toward responsibility to the survivors.
A gently mannered, plainly dressed young woman lives in the cottage beside the being's hiding place.
A deeply despondent young man lives and works with the young woman in the neighboring cottage.
A benevolent, silver-haired old man lives with the two younger cottagers and plays music for them.
A benevolent, silver-haired old man lives with the two younger cottagers and plays music for them. The elderly cottager is blind and receives devoted care from his younger companions.
A gently mannered, plainly dressed young woman lives in the cottage beside the being's hiding place. Agatha suffers severe poverty and sometimes gives her share of food to her father.
A deeply despondent young man lives and works with the young woman in the neighboring cottage. Felix suffers severe poverty and sometimes goes hungry so his father can eat.

A benevolent, silver-haired old man lives with the two younger cottagers and plays music for them. The elderly cottager is blind and receives devoted care from his younger companions. The father regularly encourages his children to resist their melancholy.

A deeply despondent young man lives and works with the young woman in the neighboring cottage. Felix suffers severe poverty and sometimes goes hungry so his father can eat. Felix tenderly eases Agatha's chores, works for a neighboring farmer, tends the garden, and reads to his family.
A veiled young woman arrives at the cottage on horseback and asks for Felix.

A deeply despondent young man lives and works with the young woman in the neighboring cottage. Felix suffers severe poverty and sometimes goes hungry so his father can eat. Felix tenderly eases Agatha's chores, works for a neighboring farmer, tends the garden, and reads to his family. Safie's arrival immediately transforms Felix's sorrow into ecstatic happiness.
A veiled young woman arrives at the cottage on horseback and asks for Felix. Safie initially cannot communicate with the cottagers but begins learning their language through instruction and signs.

A veiled young woman arrives at the cottage on horseback and asks for Felix. Safie initially cannot communicate with the cottagers but begins learning their language through instruction and signs. Safie is an exceptionally accomplished singer and guitarist.

Agatha is Felix's gentle, sad sister and the blind cottager's devoted daughter. Despite severe poverty she works tirelessly and sacrifices food for her father, then warmly welcomes Safie and helps teach her the family's language.
The blind, benevolent father of Agatha and Felix receives their devoted care, comforts them through poverty, and fills the cottage with music. He affectionately welcomes Safie when she arrives.

The being recounts his confused awakening, solitary survival, and violent rejection by villagers. Hidden beside a poor cottage, he compassionately aids its family, learns speech, literacy, history, and social customs by observing them, and longs for their acceptance; knowledge of his deformity and complete isolation increasingly turns hope into anguish.

Felix is Agatha's deeply sorrowful brother and the blind cottager's devoted son. He labors to support the impoverished household, tenderly assists his family, teaches from a history book, and becomes ecstatic when the beloved Safie arrives.

Safie is a beautiful Arabian woman whose arrival brings Felix and his household joy. Initially unable to communicate with the cottagers, she studies their language and letters, while displaying exceptional musical ability and a cheerful disposition.

Safie's father is a wealthy Turkish merchant long resident in Paris. The French government imprisons and condemns him to death, apparently because of his religion and wealth rather than a proven crime.

Safie's father is a wealthy Turkish merchant long resident in Paris. The French government imprisons and condemns him to death, apparently because of his religion and wealth rather than a proven crime. The imprisoned merchant promises Felix Safie's hand in marriage after reaching safety.

Christian Arab taught Safie her Christian faith, intellectual ambition, and independence of spirit. Safie's mother was a freeborn Christian Arab who was enslaved by the Turks before marrying Safie's father. Safie's mother died, leaving her teachings firmly impressed on her daughter.

Safie's father is a wealthy Turkish merchant long resident in Paris. The French government imprisons and condemns him to death, apparently because of his religion and wealth rather than a proven crime. The imprisoned merchant promises Felix Safie's hand in marriage after reaching safety. Despite encouraging Felix's hopes, the merchant secretly intends to prevent Safie from marrying a Christian and take her away.

Safie's father is a wealthy Turkish merchant long resident in Paris. The French government imprisons and condemns him to death, apparently because of his religion and wealth rather than a proven crime. Despite encouraging Felix's hopes, the merchant secretly intends to prevent Safie from marrying a Christian and take her away. After Felix's ruin, the merchant abandons his rescuer, leaves Italy with Safie, and sends Felix only a small sum.

Agatha once held a distinguished place in Paris but was imprisoned and permanently exiled because of Felix's rescue effort. When the being finally enters the cottage seeking acceptance, she faints at his appearance.

De Lacey is the once-prosperous French patriarch whose family lost its fortune and homeland because of Felix's rescue of Safie's father. Blind and compassionate, he offers the disguised being help before the younger cottagers interrupt them.

The being's new books and Victor's journal deepen his moral imagination, loneliness, and anguish over his origins. He carefully approaches the blind De Lacey and receives his first direct human kindness, but Felix attacks him when the family returns; devastated, he refuses to retaliate and escapes.

Felix sacrificed his family's freedom, fortune, and position to rescue Safie's unjustly condemned father, only to be betrayed by the merchant. Reunited with Safie in exile, he violently drives the being from the cottage when he finds it clinging to De Lacey.

Safie inherited Christian faith, intellectual ambition, and independence from her enslaved Arab mother. Defying her Turkish father and the prospect of confinement in Asia, she travels to Germany to reunite with Felix, but later flees in terror upon seeing the being.

Safie's wealthy Turkish merchant father receives Felix's help escaping an apparently unjust French death sentence. He secretly opposes Safie's marriage to his Christian rescuer, abandons Felix after the De Lacey family's ruin, and orders Safie to return to Turkey.
Safie's late mother was a freeborn Christian Arab enslaved by the Turks and married by Safie's father. She instilled her religion, intellectual ambition, and independence in her daughter.
Victor's abandoned creation possesses his detailed journal of the four months preceding its animation, including Victor's account of the work and horror at the resulting being.
Agatha permanently leaves the cottage with her family after fainting at the being's appearance, ending his hope of gaining her companionship.
De Lacey permanently leaves the cottage after his family fears that the being's intrusion has endangered his life, ending the being's hope of returning to seek his compassionate aid.

After the De Laceys permanently flee, the being burns their cottage and travels toward Geneva seeking redress from Victor. A man shoots him despite his rescue of a drowning girl, hardening his hatred. He murders William, frames Justine, and demands a female companion, promising that the pair will leave humanity forever; Victor accepts his condition.
Felix, now explicitly identified as Safie's husband, permanently abandons the cottage with his terrified family after the being's intrusion endangered De Lacey.

Justine's innocence is fully explained: while she slept, William's true murderer planted the stolen portrait in her clothing, creating the evidence that led to her wrongful condemnation and execution.
Safie is explicitly identified as Felix's wife and permanently leaves the cottage with his family after her terrifying encounter with the being.

After hearing that the being murdered William and framed Justine, Victor reluctantly accepts the demand to create a female companion, conditional on the pair abandoning Europe and humanity forever. He returns to Geneva resolved to complete the hated task to protect his family.
Alphonse Frankenstein is identified by William as his father and a Syndic; the being murders William after learning of the child's connection to the Frankenstein family.
William's murderer is revealed to be Victor's creation, who strangled the child after learning that he belonged to the Frankenstein family.
Elizabeth remains Victor's intended wife and helps arrange Henry's companionship for his journey, but she is deeply troubled by the prospect of Victor suffering away from her.

Henry accompanies Victor through the Rhine region, England, and Scotland, delighting in nature and intellectual society while preparing for a career connected with India. He reluctantly remains at Perth when Victor seeks solitude; Victor's present narration reveals that his beloved friend has since died.

Victor postpones marrying Elizabeth until he fulfills his agreement with the being. After secretly obtaining permission to travel, he gathers knowledge and materials in England, separates from Henry in Scotland, and advances the female creation in a remote Orkney hut while suffering intense horror and fear.
Alphonse hopes Victor will marry Elizabeth, permits his requested journey to England in expectation that travel will restore him, and arranges for Henry to accompany him.
Kirwin is the local magistrate in the Irish town where Victor lands.
The being secretly follows Victor and watches him destroy the promised female companion. Enraged by the broken agreement, he vows revenge and threatens to be with Victor on his wedding night.
Henry urges Victor to rejoin him at Perth and travel south because he must soon return to London for negotiations connected with his planned Indian enterprise.
Mr. Kirwin is the magistrate of the Irish town where Victor is taken to account for a gentleman's recent murder.

Convinced that a second creation could threaten humanity, Victor destroys the unfinished female and permanently breaks his agreement with the being. After interpreting the being's wedding-night threat as a promise to kill him, he disposes of the remains, survives being blown across the sea, lands in Ireland, and is taken to magistrate Kirwin regarding a recent murder.
Daniel testifies that he saw a lone man in a boat near the shore shortly before Henry's body was discovered.
The being is identified by Victor as Henry's murderer, adding another death to his revenge against his creator.
Daniel Nugent is a fisherman whose testimony places a lone man in Victor's boat near the location where Henry's body was found.
Henry Clerval has been murdered by strangulation in Ireland, and Victor identifies the being as his killer.
Kirwin compassionately arranges care for the imprisoned Victor, contacts his family, and organizes the evidence and defence that secure his release.

Victor discovers Henry's murdered body, nearly dies from a two-month fever, and spends three months imprisoned before an Orkney alibi clears him. Still suicidal, physically shattered, and dependent on laudanum, he sails with his father toward Geneva to protect his family and hunt the being.
Alphonse travels to Victor's Irish prison, helps his gravely ill son recover, and accompanies him by sea toward Geneva despite Victor's continuing weakness.
A criminal judge in Geneva receives Victor's accusation against the being.

A criminal judge in Geneva receives Victor's accusation against the being. The magistrate promises proper efforts to capture the being but warns that his extraordinary powers may make apprehension impossible.
The being fulfills his wedding-night threat by strangling Elizabeth rather than Victor, mocks his creator beside her corpse, and escapes into the lake; Victor now devotes himself to hunting him down.
Elizabeth affirms her selfless love for Victor, marries him in Geneva, and is strangled by the being at Evian on their wedding night.
The Genevan magistrate hears Victor's extraordinary accusation and promises proper efforts, but doubts that the being's powers and unknown location will permit his capture.

Victor marries Elizabeth while mistakenly expecting the being to attack him, then loses both her and Alphonse. After months confined as mad, he regains reason, discloses his history to a magistrate, and devotes himself entirely to destroying the being.
Alphonse continues caring devotedly for Victor, but Elizabeth's murder and the family's accumulated losses overwhelm him, and he dies in Victor's arms within days.
Ernest remains alive in Geneva after Elizabeth's murder, though Alphonse dies shortly afterward.
The expedition's surgeon treats the dying Victor and tells Walton that his friend has only hours to live.

The being deliberately sustains Victor's polar pursuit, then appears beside his dead creator in profound grief. He acknowledges Victor as his final victim and reveals that remorse, self-hatred, envy, and an irresistible appetite for vengeance accompanied his murders.
Margaret Saville remains Walton's beloved sister and correspondent in England and is now revealed to have a husband and children.

Walton becomes convinced of Victor's history and finds in him the close friend he sought, but yields to his endangered crew and turns toward England. After Victor dies, Walton encounters the grieving being and responds with mingled duty, curiosity, and compassion.

Victor pursues the being from Geneva to the polar sea, asks Walton to continue the task if necessary, helps preserve his narrative, and dies aboard the returning ship after defending his refusal to create a companion and warning Walton against ambition.
The expedition's surgeon treats the failing Victor and correctly determines that he has only hours to live.

The being admits that his crimes never satisfied his desire for love, accepts his degradation and isolation, and plans to destroy himself on a northern funeral pyre. He leaves Walton's ship on an ice raft and disappears into the darkness, though his death is not witnessed.
Walton briefly feels compassion for the grieving being but ultimately condemns his remorse as hypocritical; their confrontation ends when the being departs alone on an ice raft.

storySentences.sentence: She introduces herself as the author explaining how she conceived the story.
She began writing stories as a child and preferred privately imagining fantastic incidents.
As a girl she spent substantial time near Dundee in Scotland.
He encouraged his wife to write and pursue literary distinction.
While in Switzerland, he was writing the third canto of Childe Harold.
He proposed that each member of the group write a ghost story and began one himself.
He attempted a macabre story about a skull-headed woman but could not develop it.
He began a story based on his early experiences but soon abandoned it.
A waking nightmare of a student animating a constructed being supplied the central image for her story.
She recognized that her nightmare could become the frightening story she had sought and began writing it the next day.
He urged his wife to expand her short tale and wrote its preface.
For the 1831 edition she revised chiefly the language and left the story's substance unchanged.
He writes from St. Petersburgh to reassure his sister that his expedition has begun safely.
He hopes to explore the polar region, investigate magnetism, and possibly discover a shorter northern passage.
His library consisted of histories of voyages of discovery.
For six years he trained for the expedition through hardship, scientific study, and service on whaling voyages.
At Archangel he has hired a vessel and begun assembling a courageous crew.
At twenty-eight he is self-educated, ardent, impatient with obstacles, and painfully lonely for an understanding friend.
The English lieutenant is brave, enterprising, eager for professional advancement, and not highly cultivated.
He relinquished a desired marriage and his property so the woman he loved could marry her preferred suitor; The ship's master is gentle, honest, courageous, kindhearted, and uneducated.
He is safe, in good spirits, and already far advanced into high latitudes with a steadfast crew.
He promises Margaret that he will face the voyage's dangers prudently rather than rashly.
Walton's ship is briefly trapped in dangerous ice and dense fog far from land.
A gigantic man-shaped traveller crosses the distant ice northward in a dog-drawn sledge.
A severely weakened European is found drifting beside Walton's ship in a sledge with one surviving dog.
The shipmaster urges the stranded stranger to accept rescue aboard Walton's vessel.
The stranger boards only after learning the ship is bound for the North Pole; he is nearly frozen and emaciated.
The stranger is generally wild-eyed, melancholy, and despairing, but responds to kindness with striking gentleness.
Walton nurses the rescued stranger in his own cabin while protecting his need for rest.
The lieutenant asks why the stranger travelled so far across the ice.
The stranger crossed the ice in pursuit of someone who fled from him.
The stranger calls the gigantic traveller he pursues a daemon.
Alarmed by Walton's willingness to sacrifice everything for knowledge, he offers his own history as a warning; After recovering substantially, he watches from the deck for the preceding sledge.
He once had a beloved noble friend but now believes that he has lost everything.
He hopes his account of ruin will guide Walton away from dangers resembling his own.
Walton resolves to record the stranger's spoken narrative as faithfully as his duties allow.
Victor is Genevese by birth and belongs to a distinguished public family.
Victor's father is a respected Genevese public servant known for integrity and tireless attention to duty.
Once a prosperous merchant, Beaufort pays his debts and hides in poverty at Lucerne with his daughter.
He searches for the impoverished Beaufort intending to help him rebuild his life.
Caroline nurses her sick father and supports them through poorly paid manual work.
Beaufort dies after a prolonged decline, leaving Caroline orphaned and destitute.
He takes Caroline into his care, returns her to Geneva, and marries her two years later.
Caroline is gentle and benevolent, though her earlier hardships weakened her health.
Victor is his parents' eldest child, born in Naples and raised with exceptional tenderness.
Elizabeth is the orphaned daughter of a Milanese nobleman and a German mother who died in childbirth.
Elizabeth's father struggled for Italian liberty and was either killed or imprisoned after his property was confiscated.
Elizabeth's German mother died while giving birth to her.
With her husband's permission, Caroline brings the orphaned Elizabeth into the family home.
Elizabeth is universally loved and becomes Victor's adored childhood companion.
Elizabeth is calm and contemplative, finding delight in poetry and the grandeur of the natural world.
Victor is ardent, intensely curious, and driven to investigate the hidden causes of nature.
Henry is imaginative and adventurous, loves chivalric literature, and creates heroic songs, tales, and games; Henry Clerval is a Genevan merchant's son and Victor's closest school friend.
Victor's parents have a second son seven years younger than Victor.
Victor longs to uncover the physical and metaphysical secrets of the world rather than pursue conventional studies.
Henry dreams of becoming an adventurous benefactor remembered for heroic deeds.
Agrippa's writings ignite thirteen-year-old Victor's enthusiasm for old natural philosophy.
Albertus Magnus becomes one of the old philosophical authorities admired by young Victor.
Paracelsus becomes one of the old philosophical authorities admired by young Victor.
Victor avidly studies Agrippa, Paracelsus, and Albertus Magnus as authorities on nature's hidden powers.
Victor pursues the elixir of life in hopes of eliminating disease and vulnerability to natural death.
Victor attempts unsuccessfully to raise spirits while combining contradictory obsolete theories.
After lightning destroys an oak, the visiting philosopher explains a theory of electricity and galvanism to Victor.
The electrical explanation displaces Victor's old authorities, leading him to abandon natural philosophy temporarily for mathematics.
At seventeen, Victor is to leave Geneva and study at the University of Ingolstadt.
On her deathbed, Caroline expresses hope that Victor and Elizabeth will eventually unite and asks Elizabeth to care for the younger children.
Elizabeth becomes dangerously ill with scarlet fever but survives through Caroline's care.
Caroline dies calmly from the fever contracted while nursing Elizabeth.
Elizabeth suppresses her grief and courageously assumes responsibility for comforting and caring for the family.
Henry's father refuses to let him accompany Victor to university because he distrusts his son's ambitions and favors a commercial life.
Henry wants to accompany Victor to Ingolstadt for a liberal education and resolves not to remain confined to commerce.
Victor arrives at Ingolstadt and settles into a solitary apartment.
Krempe is an uncouth but knowledgeable professor of natural philosophy at Ingolstadt.
Krempe dismisses Victor's alchemical education as obsolete and directs him to begin his scientific studies anew.
Waldman is a benevolent chemistry professor whose appearance, voice, and teaching contrast favorably with Krempe's.
Waldman describes the practical achievements and expansive powers of modern chemistry.
Inspired by Waldman's lecture, Victor resolves to pioneer new scientific paths and uncover creation's deepest mysteries.
Waldman advises Victor to study every branch of natural philosophy, shows him his laboratory, recommends books and equipment, and offers future use of his machines.
Victor makes natural philosophy, especially chemistry, his nearly exclusive occupation and works through the night in his laboratory.
After two years, Victor's rapid progress and improvements to chemical instruments earn him esteem at the university.
Victor studies physiology, anatomy, bodily decay, and the transition between life and death in vaults and charnel houses.
Victor discovers the cause of life and becomes capable of animating lifeless matter.
Victor withholds the secret of animation from Walton and presents his own ruin as a warning about dangerous knowledge.
Victor begins constructing a human being about eight feet tall after months of collecting and arranging materials.
Victor hopes to create a grateful new species and eventually restore life to bodies claimed by death.
Victor obtains bodily materials from charnel houses, dissecting rooms, and slaughterhouses while bringing his creation toward completion.
Victor's obsessive labor leaves him pale and emaciated and causes him to neglect nature, correspondence, and distant loved ones.
As his creation nears completion, Victor suffers fever, painful nervousness, isolation, and anxiety but continues working.
Victor's father responds to his prolonged silence without reproach but asks increasingly detailed questions about his occupations.
The constructed being opens an eye, breathes, and begins moving after Victor gives it life.
Victor successfully animates the human-shaped body he constructed.
The being has yellow skin, black hair and lips, white teeth, watery eyes, and a shrivelled complexion that Victor finds horrifying.
The being approaches Victor's bed, makes inarticulate sounds, and reaches toward him before Victor escapes.
Victor recoils from his creation in horror and abandons the laboratory.
Henry arrives in Ingolstadt and reunites with Victor.
Henry's father overcomes his dislike of learning and permits Henry to study at Ingolstadt.
Henry's persistent entreaties persuade his father to permit his university education.
The being leaves Victor's apartment and its whereabouts become unknown to him.
Henry becomes Victor's sole nurse and conceals the severity of his illness from Victor's distant family.
After a fit, Victor develops a nervous fever that confines him for several months.
Victor slowly recovers through the spring after frequent relapses.
Elizabeth restrains Victor's father from making the long journey to Ingolstadt while anxiously awaiting proof of Victor's recovery.
Victor's father remains healthy but opposes Ernest pursuing a military career abroad.
Ernest is sixteen, active and spirited, dislikes sustained study, and prefers outdoor pursuits; Ernest wants to enter foreign military service despite his family's reluctance.
Justine is the third of the Moritz family's four children and entered Victor's household at age twelve after Caroline intervened against her mother's mistreatment.
M. Moritz dies after having favored Justine among his children.
Madame Moritz mistreated Justine after her husband's death and eventually allowed Caroline to take the girl into her household.
Caroline becomes attached to Justine and provides her with a better education than originally intended.
Justine is grateful, frank, cheerful, and devoted to Caroline, whose speech and manners she imitates.
Justine attends Caroline with anxious devotion during Caroline's fatal illness.
Grief has softened Justine's former vivacity; Elizabeth now finds her clever, gentle, and very pretty; After her mother's death, Justine returns to Victor's family in Geneva.

After her other children die, Madame Moritz recalls Justine home but alternates between asking forgiveness and blaming her for their deaths; Madame Moritz dies after a decline during the previous winter.
William is tall for his age, healthy and cheerful, with blue eyes, curling hair, and dimples; William is Victor's young brother living with the family in Geneva.
Victor writes home to reassure his family despite the effort greatly fatiguing him.
Henry removes Victor's scientific apparatus, changes his room, and tactfully redirects conversations that distress him.
Victor develops a violent aversion to natural philosophy, laboratories, and chemical instruments because they revive his nervous agony.
Krempe now considers Victor the university's leading scientific student and praises his advancement.
Henry studies Persian, Arabic, and Sanskrit to prepare for an enterprising career in the East.
Victor joins Henry's oriental-language studies for consolation and relief from his former scientific pursuits.
Victor longs to return to Geneva but postpones leaving until Henry is established in Ingolstadt, with winter delaying him further.
A walking tour with Henry fully restores Victor's health, sociability, affection for nature, and cheerful spirits.
Henry sustains Victor's recovery with affectionate companionship, imaginative stories, poetry, and conversation.
Ernest searches unsuccessfully for William after the younger boy hides during their game.
Alphonse searches through the night and finds William's strangled body on the grass at dawn.
William has been murdered by strangulation near Plainpalais.
Elizabeth examines William's body and blames herself for his death because she let him wear Caroline's valuable miniature.
After learning of William's murder, Victor decides to return to Geneva immediately.
Henry grieves for William and offers Victor heartfelt sympathy before Victor departs.
The being appears near Plainpalais during a storm, rapidly scales Mont Salêve, and disappears over its summit.
Seeing his creation near the murder site convinces Victor that the being killed William.
Victor conceals his suspicions because his account would seem insane and the being would be nearly impossible to capture.
Alphonse is overwhelmed and physically diminished by William's death.
Justine is arrested for William's murder after Caroline's missing miniature is found in her pocket and her confusion heightens suspicion.
Victor remains certain that Justine is innocent despite the evidence against her.
Elizabeth firmly believes Justine innocent and hopes Victor can save her from conviction.
Justine explains that she spent the murder night searching for William and trapped outside Geneva, but cannot explain how the miniature entered her pocket.
Elizabeth testifies publicly to Justine's benevolence, devoted caregiving, affection for William, and innocence.
The court unanimously condemns Justine for William's murder.
Justine reveals that her confession was false and made under religious coercion to obtain absolution.
Justine's confessor threatens her with excommunication and damnation until she makes a false confession.
Comforted that Victor and Elizabeth believe her, Justine becomes resigned and faces death with courage.
Elizabeth's impassioned attempts to save Justine fail to change the judges' decision.
Justine is executed on the scaffold after the judges reject appeals for her innocence.
Victor appeals indignantly for Justine but withholds his intended disclosure when he realizes it would not overturn her sentence.
Victor is consumed by remorse, horror, and despair because he regards William and Justine as victims of his creation.
Victor's guilt and remorse after Justine's death leave him sleepless, despairing, and unable to recover his former benevolent hopes.
Alphonse urges Victor to moderate his grief for the sake of himself and the surviving family.
Victor considers drowning himself in the lake but remains alive to protect Elizabeth, Alphonse, and Ernest from his creation.
Victor longs to destroy his creation and avenge William and Justine.
Elizabeth becomes despondent, abandons her ordinary pleasures, and regards prolonged mourning as a tribute to the innocent dead.
Alphonse's health is deeply shaken by William's murder and Justine's execution.
Justine's wrongful execution destroys Elizabeth's former confidence in human justice and worldly happiness.
Elizabeth comforts Victor and urges him to abandon despair and revenge for the sake of the friends who love him.
The grandeur of the Alpine landscape temporarily subdues Victor's grief and brings him limited peace.
Seeking relief from despair, Victor travels alone through the Alpine valleys to Chamounix and Montanvert.
The being crosses the glacier toward Victor with superhuman speed and agility.
The being can speak fluently, reason about his condition, and demand duties from his creator.
Victor attacks the being in an attempt to destroy him, but the being easily evades him.
The being is taller, stronger, and more agile than Victor, though he initially refuses to fight his creator.
Rejected by humanity, the being has wandered for days among desert mountains and glaciers and sheltered in ice caves.
The being asks Victor to hear his history before deciding whether to pity or destroy him.
Victor recognizes for the first time that a creator may owe duties to his creature and should seek its happiness before condemning its conduct.
After awakening with confused senses, the being wanders into the forest near Ingolstadt and survives on berries and brook water.
The being discovers that fire supplies warmth but can also burn him.
Villagers react to his appearance with terror and violence, forcing him to flee bruised into a hovel adjoining a cottage.
A gently mannered, plainly dressed young woman lives in the cottage beside the being's hiding place.
A deeply despondent young man lives and works with the young woman in the neighboring cottage.
A benevolent, silver-haired old man lives with the two younger cottagers and plays music for them.
The elderly cottager is blind and receives devoted care from his younger companions.
Agatha suffers severe poverty and sometimes gives her share of food to her father.
Felix suffers severe poverty and sometimes goes hungry so his father can eat.
After recognizing the family's hunger, the being stops stealing their food and forages elsewhere.
The being secretly gathers several days of firewood to reduce Felix's labor.
Through prolonged observation, the being begins learning spoken language and discovers the cottagers' names.
The father regularly encourages his children to resist their melancholy.
Felix tenderly eases Agatha's chores, works for a neighboring farmer, tends the garden, and reads to his family.
Seeing his reflection makes the being understand the extreme deformity of his appearance.
The being hopes to approach the cottagers gently, win their acceptance, and eventually earn their love.
A veiled young woman arrives at the cottage on horseback and asks for Felix.
Safie's arrival immediately transforms Felix's sorrow into ecstatic happiness.
Safie initially cannot communicate with the cottagers but begins learning their language through instruction and signs.
Safie is an exceptionally accomplished singer and guitarist.
By secretly sharing Safie's lessons, the being learns to understand most of the cottagers' speech within two months.
The being also learns written language from Felix's lessons to Safie.
Felix's explanations of history teach the being about nations, governments, religions, empires, and human violence.
Learning how society values wealth and ancestry intensifies the being's awareness that he has neither property, friends, kin, nor any known equal.
Greater knowledge deepens the being's anguish and his frustrated longing to join human society.
After learning about human families, the being recognizes that he remembers no parents, relations, childhood, or other being like himself.
The being privately regards the cottagers as his protectors and feels increasing love and reverence for them.
Before her family's ruin, Agatha held a distinguished social position in Paris.
De Lacey came from a respected French family and formerly lived prosperously in Paris.
Before his family's ruin, Felix was educated for service to France.

Safie's father is a wealthy Turkish merchant long resident in Paris; The French government imprisons and condemns him to death, apparently because of his religion and wealth rather than a proven crime.
Outraged by the merchant's sentence, Felix vows to free him and secretly arranges his escape.
The imprisoned merchant promises Felix Safie's hand in marriage after reaching safety.
Safie sends Felix translated letters expressing gratitude for his aid and sorrow over her own future.
Safie longs to marry a Christian and remain where women may participate in society rather than return to confinement in Asia.
Safie's mother was a freeborn Christian Arab who was enslaved by the Turks before marrying Safie's father; Safie's mother died, leaving her teachings firmly impressed on her daughter.
De Lacey assists Felix's escape plan by hiding in Paris with Agatha.
Felix frees the merchant and conducts the fugitives from Paris through France and across the Alps to Leghorn.
Despite encouraging Felix's hopes, the merchant secretly intends to prevent Safie from marrying a Christian and take her away.
French authorities imprison Agatha and her father after discovering Felix's role in the escape.
French authorities imprison De Lacey and Agatha after discovering Felix's role in the escape.
Felix leaves Safie and surrenders to French authorities in a failed attempt to free his father and sister.
After five months of confinement, De Lacey's family loses its fortune and is permanently exiled from France.
After Felix's ruin, the merchant abandons his rescuer, leaves Italy with Safie, and sends Felix only a small sum.
Felix's misery arose chiefly from the merchant's ingratitude and his apparent loss of Safie, whose return restores his happiness.
Defying her father's command to return to Turkey, Safie takes her own jewels and money and travels toward Felix's German home.
Safie devotedly nurses her travelling attendant through a fatal illness and then reaches Felix's cottage with local help.
The cottagers' history deepens the being's admiration for virtue and his condemnation of human vice.
The being finds a portmanteau containing Paradise Lost, Plutarch's Lives, and The Sorrows of Werther, which he studies continually.
Reading Paradise Lost leads the being to compare his isolation with Adam's and Satan's, intensifying his loneliness and envy.
Victor's journal teaches the being the circumstances of his creation and confirms that his creator found his appearance repulsive.
Victor kept a detailed journal describing the four months before the being's creation and his horror at its appearance.
The being resolves to prepare carefully for an interview in which he hopes to win the cottagers' compassion and friendship.
The being plans to approach De Lacey alone, hoping the blind father's judgment will not be influenced by his appearance.
Unable to see the being's appearance, De Lacey trusts his sincerity and offers to help reconcile him with his supposed friends.
De Lacey becomes the first person to address the being with direct kindness, giving him confidence that the family may accept him.
Agatha faints in horror when she sees the being clinging to her father.
Though strong enough to kill Felix, the devastated being refuses to retaliate and escapes to his hovel.
Felix tears the being away from De Lacey, knocks him down, and beats him with a stick.
Safie flees the cottage in terror upon seeing the being.
After his rejection, the being declares permanent war on humanity and especially on Victor.
Agatha leaves the cottage permanently with her family after the terrifying encounter with the being.
De Lacey leaves the cottage permanently after the family fears that his encounter with the being has endangered his life.
Felix permanently abandons the cottage with his family because their encounter with the being has endangered and terrified them.
Safie leaves the cottage permanently with Felix and his family after the terrifying encounter with the being.
Enraged by the family's departure, the being destroys their garden and burns their abandoned cottage.
The being resolves to travel to Geneva and demand pity and redress from Victor, his creator.
The being rescues a drowning girl and attempts to revive her; A man shoots the being in the shoulder after taking the rescued girl from him.
After learning that William belongs to the Frankenstein family, the being strangles him as his first victim of revenge.
The being is revealed as William's murderer.
The being plants the portrait taken from William in the sleeping Justine's clothing to make her suffer for the murder.
The being framed the sleeping Justine by placing William's stolen portrait in her clothing.
The being demands that Victor create a female of his own kind to end his isolation.
The being promises to withdraw permanently from human society with a female companion and live harmlessly in a remote wilderness.
Victor agrees to create a female companion for the being on condition that the pair leave Europe and human society forever.
The being solemnly accepts Victor's condition and promises to watch for the completion of his companion.
Victor returns to Geneva resolved to undertake the abhorrent creation in hopes of protecting his family.
Victor's health and spirits improve, though thoughts of his promise still drive him into periods of melancholy and solitude.
Victor declares that his future hopes depend on eventually marrying Elizabeth.
Alphonse hopes Victor will marry Elizabeth and asks whether the marriage should take place immediately.
Victor conceals his true purpose and obtains his father's consent to visit England for up to a year.
Alphonse arranges for Henry to accompany Victor on the journey from Strasbourg.
Elizabeth helps arrange for Henry to accompany Victor so that he will not travel alone; Elizabeth is deeply uneasy about Victor's suffering during their separation and bids him a tearful farewell.
Henry possesses an enthusiastic imagination, deep sensitivity, devoted affection, and an ardent love of nature.
Victor's present-day narration reveals that Henry has since died.
In London Victor consults distinguished natural philosophers to obtain knowledge needed for the promised creation.
Henry plans to visit India and use his linguistic and social knowledge to advance European colonization and trade.
Victor begins gathering the materials needed to construct the female being.
Fearing that the being may murder Henry, Victor stays close to his friend to protect him.
Henry reluctantly accepts Victor's solitary Scottish tour and asks him to write often and return quickly.
Victor parts from Henry to find a remote Scottish location where he can finish the female creation alone.
Victor settles in a rented hut on a remote, barren Orkney island to conduct his work.
The solitary work makes Victor restless and nervous, and he constantly fears the being's appearance.
Victor's construction of the female being is considerably advanced despite his horror and intermittent reluctance.
Victor recognizes that creating a female being could endanger humanity through her independent violence or the propagation of a new species.
The being has secretly followed Victor throughout his travels and appears at the laboratory window to inspect his progress.
The destruction of the unfinished female ends the being's hope of receiving the promised companion and provokes despair and revenge.
Victor tears apart the unfinished female creation while the being watches.
Victor vows never to resume construction of the female being.
The being vows revenge and threatens to be present on Victor's wedding night.
Victor resolves to resist the being bitterly rather than submit to his expected attack; Victor interprets the wedding-night threat as notice that the being intends to kill him then.
Henry asks Victor to meet him at Perth and travel south before Henry returns to London for negotiations connected with his Indian enterprise.
Encouraged by Henry's letter, Victor decides to leave the island within two days and rejoin him.
Victor sinks the unfinished creation's remains at sea to prevent their discovery by the islanders.
After falling asleep in his skiff, Victor is driven far offshore without a compass and fears death from starvation or drowning.
Victor discovers that the unfamiliar coastal town where he has landed is in Ireland.
Kirwin is the local magistrate in the Irish town where Victor lands.
Exhausted and hungry, Victor is taken to Kirwin's house to account for a gentleman murdered the previous night.
The description of the victim's strangulation reminds Victor of William's murder and visibly agitates him, increasing suspicion against him.
Daniel testifies that he saw a lone man in a boat near the shore shortly before Henry's body was discovered.
Henry is found murdered by strangulation, with finger marks around his neck.
After seeing Henry's corpse, Victor suffers convulsions followed by a fever that leaves him near death for two months.
Victor regains awareness after two months and finds himself confined in prison on the murder charge.
Kirwin arranges the best available prison room, a physician, and a nurse for the gravely ill Victor and checks that he is not neglected.
Kirwin examines Victor's papers and writes to his family in Geneva after finding a letter from his father.
Alphonse travels from Geneva to visit Victor in the Irish prison after learning of his illness and arrest.
Alphonse's presence and care help Victor gradually recover his physical health.
Although physically recovering, Victor remains profoundly melancholic, repeatedly wishes for death, and obsesses over Henry's murder.
Kirwin gathers witnesses and organizes Victor's legal defence.
Evidence that Victor was on the Orkney Islands when Henry's body was found causes the grand jury to reject the charge, and he is released after three months' imprisonment.
Victor's despair leads him to attempt self-harm, requiring constant supervision to restrain him.
Victor resolves to return quickly to Geneva, protect his loved ones, and kill the being if he finds him.
Victor identifies the being as responsible for Henry's murder.
Despite concerns about Victor's weakness, Alphonse yields to his urgency and sails with him from Ireland toward Havre-de-Grace.
Victor relies nightly on laudanum for sleep and remains tormented by nightmares of the being attacking him.
Alphonse tirelessly cares for the exhausted Victor during their stay in Paris.
Elizabeth declares her love but asks Victor to marry her only if the choice is freely his, placing his happiness above her own hopes.
Believing the wedding-night threat concerns his own death, Victor resolves not to postpone marriage if it will make Elizabeth and Alphonse happy.
Victor constantly carries pistols and a dagger while watching for an attack by the being.
Elizabeth is murdered in her room at the Evian inn on her wedding night.
The being strangles Elizabeth on her wedding night.
The being evades Victor's shot, leaps from the window, and escapes into the lake.
Victor fires at the being after seeing him mockingly indicate Elizabeth's corpse.
Fearing that the being may also attack Alphonse and Ernest, Victor resolves to return immediately to Geneva.
Overcome by Elizabeth's murder and the family's accumulated losses, Alphonse dies in Victor's arms within a few days.
Ernest is still alive when Victor returns to Geneva after Elizabeth's murder.
After Alphonse's death, Victor is judged mad, confined alone for many months, and eventually released when his reason returns.
On recovering his reason, Victor becomes consumed by the desire to seize and take revenge on the being.
A criminal judge in Geneva receives Victor's accusation against the being.
Victor discloses his history to the magistrate calmly and precisely while requesting the being's capture and punishment.
The magistrate promises proper efforts to capture the being but warns that his extraordinary powers may make apprehension impossible.
Convinced official help will fail, Victor devotes his life or death to destroying the being himself.
Victor leaves Geneva forever with money and jewels that belonged to his mother.
At his family's graves, Victor swears to preserve his life and pursue the being until one of them dies.
The being overhears Victor's vow, mocks him, and declares satisfaction that he has chosen to live.
Victor follows the being for months through the Rhône region, the Black Sea, Tartary, and Russia.
The being deliberately leaves tracks, written taunts, and occasional food so Victor will continue the pursuit northward.
At a northern hamlet, the being seizes winter provisions, trained dogs, and a sledge before travelling across the frozen sea.
After approaching within a mile of the being, Victor is separated from him by breaking ice and left drifting toward death on an ice fragment.
Victor reveals that Walton's ship rescued him while he was pursuing the being across the polar ice.
Victor asks Walton to kill the being if it appears after Victor's death, while acknowledging that he cannot demand Walton undertake the same pursuit.
Felix and Safie's letters and the expedition's sighting of the being convince Walton that Victor's narrative is true.
Victor reviews Walton's notes of his history and corrects and expands them to preserve a fuller account.
Despite his ruin, Victor displays extensive literary knowledge, keen understanding, and powerful eloquence.
Walton recognizes Victor as the sympathetic friend he long sought and fears losing him almost immediately.
Walton's ship is trapped among threatening ice, and he blames his ambitions for endangering the crew.
Margaret is revealed to have a husband and children in England.
Though gravely ill, Victor delivers an impassioned speech urging Walton's frightened sailors to remain steadfast in the expedition.
Walton consents to turn south if the ship escapes the ice, sacrificing his expedition's intended achievement to his crew's demand.
Victor refuses to abandon his own pursuit when Walton turns south, but collapses unconscious while trying to rise from bed.
The expedition's surgeon treats the dying Victor and tells Walton that his friend has only hours to live.
Near death, Victor recognizes that creating the being imposed a duty to promote its welfare, but maintains that his duty to humanity justified refusing it a companion.
Victor bids Walton farewell and advises him to seek tranquil happiness and avoid ambition.
Victor dies peacefully aboard Walton's ship after feebly pressing his friend's hand.
Walton grieves deeply for Victor while continuing the return journey toward England.
The being boards Walton's ship and mourns over Victor's remains in the cabin.
The being regards Victor as his final victim, acknowledges destroying him through the deaths of his loved ones, and vainly asks the dead creator's pardon.
Walton confronts the being but suspends his impulse to kill him when curiosity and compassion arise at his grief.
The being says that remorse and anguish accompanied his revenge, including Henry's murder, even while selfish hatred drove him onward.
After Henry's murder, the being pitied Victor and loathed himself, but envy of Victor's hoped-for happiness renewed his resolve to fulfill the wedding-night threat.
Walton rejects the being's apparent grief as hypocrisy and accuses him of mourning only because Victor is beyond further vengeance.
The being says his former hopes for virtue, affection, honor, and acceptance have been destroyed by crime and despair, leaving him utterly alone.
The being says that destroying Victor's hopes never satisfied his continuing desire for love and fellowship, and he still resents humanity's rejection of him.
The being admits murdering helpless innocents and pursuing Victor to death, while declaring that his own self-abhorrence exceeds Walton's hatred.
The being intends to travel north, build his own funeral pyre, and burn himself so his remains cannot enable anyone to create another being like him.
After bidding farewell, the being leaps from the cabin onto an ice raft and disappears into darkness and distance.
